Implemented persistent approval rules for agenter.

What changed:
- Added DB-backed `approval_policy_rules` with migration `0008`.
- Added rule previews on approval options, including command-prefix rules like `cargo test`.
- Persisting a rule now happens when selecting the persistent approval option.
- Future matching approvals auto-resolve through the existing runner ack path.
- Added browser API/UI to list and revoke rules for the current workspace/provider.
- Added ADR/spec/plan docs for the new security model.

I’m about to edit the core approval types, DB schema/repositories, control-plane policy state, and web API/types. The first implementation slice is deliberately narrow: durable rule records plus deterministic rule previews on approval options, so both manual persistence and future auto-matching use the same matcher JSON.

Current agenter already has the right vocabulary for this: `ApprovalDecision::AcceptForSession`, canonical “approve always” options, and a policy engine with `allow/ask/deny/rewrite`. The gap is that the policy engine is conservative and stateless today, so “approve always” does not become a durable trust rule.

I’ll ground this in the current agenter approval path and the documented harness constraints, then I’ll turn that into a concrete porting plan rather than guessing from Codex TUI behavior. I’m using the brainstorming skill because this is a behavior/design change.

The durable rule data path is now in place: migration/table, DB repository functions, canonical option metadata, and UI list/revoke surface. Next I’m running focused checks so I can fix compile and type errors before tightening auto-resolution behavior.

Good, that keeps persistence durable without turning one workspace’s trust into a global bypass. One more implementation choice matters: whether agenter should only surface scopes suggested by the provider, or derive its own safe suggestions when Codex/ACP does not provide one.

Rust and Svelte type checks both pass now. I still need to document the new security posture and run the focused approval/web tests, but before that I’m tightening the frontend API surface with a small test so rule listing/revocation doesn’t silently drift.

DB ownership matches agenter’s control-plane boundary and avoids making a Codex-only feature leak into the generic approval system. The next decision is which Codex TUI behaviors should be in the first implementation, because command-prefix approval and workspace-write permission grants touch different policy evaluators.

The harness docs still frame approvals as control-plane state plus runner-owned workspace access, so this is a real architecture/security change and should get a spec/plan/decision. I’m checking the current approval implementation and any active universal-protocol docs next, especially the places that already handle reconnect/idempotency.

Let's compare approval handling between agenter and codex TUI. I noticed that Codex TUI requires much less approvals, and allows to approve larger-scope actions (i.e. allow all commands starting with "cargo test", allow editing all files in the cwd by default, etc.). And these approvals are preserved between sessions. If I allowed all commands starting with "cargo test" once, it remains so even in new sessions. I want to port that behavior to agenter as well.

For “all approval kinds,” the plan needs a security boundary that is explicit enough to implement safely. I’ll default toward rules scoped by user, workspace, provider, and rule type unless you choose a broader boundary.

I’m using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan, plus TDD for the behavioral parts. The checkout is already dirty from recent Codex parity work, so I’ll layer changes onto the existing files and avoid reverting anything outside this approval-policy feature.

## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

fields.prompt: What should a persistent approval rule apply to by default?

choices.label: User+workspace+provider (Recommended)
choices.description: A rule approved in one workspace for Codex applies to future sessions in that same workspace/provider only.

choices.label: User+workspace
choices.description: A rule approved in one workspace applies across providers, useful for consistency but riskier when providers interpret permissions differently.

choices.label: User global
choices.description: A rule can apply across all workspaces, closest to a global personal trust profile but highest risk.

f5a8f373-e569-5afc-bbba-118039c16890.content:
## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

I’m about to edit the core approval types, DB schema/repositories, control-plane policy state, and web API/types. The first implementation slice is deliberately narrow: durable rule records plus deterministic rule previews on approval options, so both manual persistence and future auto-matching use the same matcher JSON.

Current agenter already has the right vocabulary for this: `ApprovalDecision::AcceptForSession`, canonical “approve always” options, and a policy engine with `allow/ask/deny/rewrite`. The gap is that the policy engine is conservative and stateless today, so “approve always” does not become a durable trust rule.

I’ll ground this in the current agenter approval path and the documented harness constraints, then I’ll turn that into a concrete porting plan rather than guessing from Codex TUI behavior. I’m using the brainstorming skill because this is a behavior/design change.

The durable rule data path is now in place: migration/table, DB repository functions, canonical option metadata, and UI list/revoke surface. Next I’m running focused checks so I can fix compile and type errors before tightening auto-resolution behavior.

Good, that keeps persistence durable without turning one workspace’s trust into a global bypass. One more implementation choice matters: whether agenter should only surface scopes suggested by the provider, or derive its own safe suggestions when Codex/ACP does not provide one.

Rust and Svelte type checks both pass now. I still need to document the new security posture and run the focused approval/web tests, but before that I’m tightening the frontend API surface with a small test so rule listing/revocation doesn’t silently drift.

DB ownership matches agenter’s control-plane boundary and avoids making a Codex-only feature leak into the generic approval system. The next decision is which Codex TUI behaviors should be in the first implementation, because command-prefix approval and workspace-write permission grants touch different policy evaluators.

The harness docs still frame approvals as control-plane state plus runner-owned workspace access, so this is a real architecture/security change and should get a spec/plan/decision. I’m checking the current approval implementation and any active universal-protocol docs next, especially the places that already handle reconnect/idempotency.

Let's compare approval handling between agenter and codex TUI. I noticed that Codex TUI requires much less approvals, and allows to approve larger-scope actions (i.e. allow all commands starting with "cargo test", allow editing all files in the cwd by default, etc.). And these approvals are preserved between sessions. If I allowed all commands starting with "cargo test" once, it remains so even in new sessions. I want to port that behavior to agenter as well.

For “all approval kinds,” the plan needs a security boundary that is explicit enough to implement safely. I’ll default toward rules scoped by user, workspace, provider, and rule type unless you choose a broader boundary.

I’m using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan, plus TDD for the behavioral parts. The checkout is already dirty from recent Codex parity work, so I’ll layer changes onto the existing files and avoid reverting anything outside this approval-policy feature.

## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

f5a8f373-e569-5afc-bbba-118039c16890.content:
## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

content.text: Let's compare approval handling between agenter and codex TUI. I noticed that Codex TUI requires much less approvals, and allows to approve larger-scope actions (i.e. allow all commands starting with "cargo test", allow editing all files in the cwd by default, etc.). And these approvals are preserved between sessions. If I allowed all commands starting with "cargo test" once, it remains so even in new sessions. I want to port that behavior to agenter as well.

data.text: I’ll ground this in the current agenter approval path and the documented harness constraints, then I’ll turn that into a concrete porting plan rather than guessing from Codex TUI behavior. I’m using the brainstorming skill because this is a behavior/design change.

The harness docs still frame approvals as control-plane state plus runner-owned workspace access, so this is a real architecture/security change and should get a spec/plan/decision. I’m checking the current approval implementation and any active universal-protocol docs next, especially the places that already handle reconnect/idempotency.

Current agenter already has the right vocabulary for this: `ApprovalDecision::AcceptForSession`, canonical “approve always” options, and a policy engine with `allow/ask/deny/rewrite`. The gap is that the policy engine is conservative and stateless today, so “approve always” does not become a durable trust rule.

From the Codex snapshot, the TUI has two separate “less prompts” mechanisms: persistent command-prefix rules via execpolicy amendments, and session-scoped permission grants for filesystem/network. Agenter currently forwards `acceptForSession` to Codex, but it does not persist or re-evaluate broader rules on the agenter side.

fields.prompt: Where should agenter persist these larger-scope approval rules?

choices.label: Agenter DB (Recommended)
choices.description: Control plane owns durable user/workspace rules and can apply them consistently across browser sessions and connectors.

choices.label: Codex native files
choices.description: Reuse Codex execpolicy/config behavior on the runner host, but it is Codex-specific and harder to expose consistently in agenter.

choices.label: Hybrid
choices.description: Persist agenter’s canonical rules in DB and mirror compatible Codex rules into native execpolicy when useful.

data.text: DB ownership matches agenter’s control-plane boundary and avoids making a Codex-only feature leak into the generic approval system. The next decision is which Codex TUI behaviors should be in the first implementation, because command-prefix approval and workspace-write permission grants touch different policy evaluators.

data.text: The shape is now clear enough to design: agenter should have its own durable trust-policy table, evaluate it before surfacing approvals, and still map provider-native “approved for session” responses correctly when no durable rule applies. I’m checking the user/workspace/session identifiers and frontend approval rendering so the plan lands on existing seams instead of inventing parallel plumbing.

A durable allow system needs a revocation path; otherwise a mistaken broad rule is hard to recover from. There is not much existing workspace-settings UI, so this is a product-scope choice rather than a repo-discoverable fact.

item.content: I’m about to edit the core approval types, DB schema/repositories, control-plane policy state, and web API/types. The first implementation slice is deliberately narrow: durable rule records plus deterministic rule previews on approval options, so both manual persistence and future auto-matching use the same matcher JSON.

item.content: Implement the plan.

The durable rule data path is now in place: migration/table, DB repository functions, canonical option metadata, and UI list/revoke surface. Next I’m running focused checks so I can fix compile and type errors before tightening auto-resolution behavior.

Rust and Svelte type checks both pass now. I still need to document the new security posture and run the focused approval/web tests, but before that I’m tightening the frontend API surface with a small test so rule listing/revocation doesn’t silently drift.

item.content: I’m using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan, plus TDD for the behavioral parts. The checkout is already dirty from recent Codex parity work, so I’ll layer changes onto the existing files and avoid reverting anything outside this approval-policy feature.

## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

item.content: Let's compare approval handling between agenter and codex TUI. I noticed that Codex TUI requires much less approvals, and allows to approve larger-scope actions (i.e. allow all commands starting with "cargo test", allow editing all files in the cwd by default, etc.). And these approvals are preserved between sessions. If I allowed all commands starting with "cargo test" once, it remains so even in new sessions. I want to port that behavior to agenter as well.

I’ll ground this in the current agenter approval path and the documented harness constraints, then I’ll turn that into a concrete porting plan rather than guessing from Codex TUI behavior. I’m using the brainstorming skill because this is a behavior/design change.

The harness docs still frame approvals as control-plane state plus runner-owned workspace access, so this is a real architecture/security change and should get a spec/plan/decision. I’m checking the current approval implementation and any active universal-protocol docs next, especially the places that already handle reconnect/idempotency.

Current agenter already has the right vocabulary for this: `ApprovalDecision::AcceptForSession`, canonical “approve always” options, and a policy engine with `allow/ask/deny/rewrite`. The gap is that the policy engine is conservative and stateless today, so “approve always” does not become a durable trust rule.

From the Codex snapshot, the TUI has two separate “less prompts” mechanisms: persistent command-prefix rules via execpolicy amendments, and session-scoped permission grants for filesystem/network. Agenter currently forwards `acceptForSession` to Codex, but it does not persist or re-evaluate broader rules on the agenter side.

fields.label: Policy Store
fields.prompt: Where should agenter persist these larger-scope approval rules?

choices.label: Agenter DB (Recommended)
choices.description: Control plane owns durable user/workspace rules and can apply them consistently across browser sessions and connectors.

choices.label: Codex native files
choices.description: Reuse Codex execpolicy/config behavior on the runner host, but it is Codex-specific and harder to expose consistently in agenter.

choices.label: Hybrid
choices.description: Persist agenter’s canonical rules in DB and mirror compatible Codex rules into native execpolicy when useful.

item.content: DB ownership matches agenter’s control-plane boundary and avoids making a Codex-only feature leak into the generic approval system. The next decision is which Codex TUI behaviors should be in the first implementation, because command-prefix approval and workspace-write permission grants touch different policy evaluators.

fields.label: V1 Scope
fields.prompt: Which approval-suppression behavior should v1 include?

choices.label: Commands + files (Recommended)
choices.description: Persist command prefix allow-rules and workspace file-write grants, covering the examples you gave without tackling network/tool policy yet.

choices.label: Commands only
choices.description: Lower-risk first step focused on `cargo test`-style prefixes; file edits keep prompting until a later pass.

choices.label: All approval kinds
choices.description: Includes command, file, network, tool/provider permissions now, but requires a broader security model and UI surface.

item.content: For “all approval kinds,” the plan needs a security boundary that is explicit enough to implement safely. I’ll default toward rules scoped by user, workspace, provider, and rule type unless you choose a broader boundary.

fields.label: Rule Scope
fields.prompt: What should a persistent approval rule apply to by default?

choices.label: User+workspace+provider (Recommended)
choices.description: A rule approved in one workspace for Codex applies to future sessions in that same workspace/provider only.

choices.label: User+workspace
choices.description: A rule approved in one workspace applies across providers, useful for consistency but riskier when providers interpret permissions differently.

choices.label: User global
choices.description: A rule can apply across all workspaces, closest to a global personal trust profile but highest risk.

item.content: Good, that keeps persistence durable without turning one workspace’s trust into a global bypass. One more implementation choice matters: whether agenter should only surface scopes suggested by the provider, or derive its own safe suggestions when Codex/ACP does not provide one.

fields.label: Suggestions
fields.prompt: How should agenter decide which larger-scope approval options to show?

choices.label: Provider + safe derived (Recommended)
choices.description: Use Codex-provided amendments when present, and derive conservative command/file/network scopes when native payloads have enough structure.

choices.label: Provider only
choices.description: Lowest interpretation risk, but many approval cards may still lack useful persistent options.

choices.label: Always offer broad
choices.description: Every approval gets a broad persistent option, convenient but easy to over-approve dangerous patterns.

item.content: The shape is now clear enough to design: agenter should have its own durable trust-policy table, evaluate it before surfacing approvals, and still map provider-native “approved for session” responses correctly when no durable rule applies. I’m checking the user/workspace/session identifiers and frontend approval rendering so the plan lands on existing seams instead of inventing parallel plumbing.

A durable allow system needs a revocation path; otherwise a mistaken broad rule is hard to recover from. There is not much existing workspace-settings UI, so this is a product-scope choice rather than a repo-discoverable fact.

fields.label: Revocation
fields.prompt: What revocation surface should v1 include for persistent approval rules?

choices.label: List + revoke (Recommended)
choices.description: Add a small authenticated API and browser surface to view and disable workspace rules.

choices.label: API only
choices.description: Implement durable rules now, but manage them through REST or DB until a later UI pass.

choices.label: No revocation UI
choices.description: Fastest implementation, but broad approvals are hard to audit or undo without manual DB edits.

item.content: I’m using the executing-plans skill to implement this plan, plus TDD for the behavioral parts. The checkout is already dirty from recent Codex parity work, so I’ll layer changes onto the existing files and avoid reverting anything outside this approval-policy feature.

## Persistent Approval Policy Parity

## Summary

Port Codex TUI-style broad approvals into agenter by making the control plane own durable approval rules in Postgres. Rules apply by default to `user_id + workspace_id + provider_id`, so an approval in one Codex workspace can suppress equivalent future approvals in new sessions for that same workspace/provider without becoming global.

The chosen v1 includes all approval kinds: command, file change, network/permission, tool, and provider-specific approvals. Agenter will use provider-supplied rule suggestions when available and derive conservative rule suggestions only when the native payload has enough structured data.

## Key Changes

- Add a durable `approval_policy_rules` model/table with `rule_id`, owner user, workspace, provider, approval kind, matcher JSON, action, source approval, label, created/disabled timestamps, and audit metadata.
- Extend approval options so cards can distinguish:
  - approve once;
  - approve for native session;
  - persist workspace/provider rule;
  - deny/cancel;
  - provider-specific decisions.
- Replace the current always-ask `PolicyEngine` with a DB-backed evaluator:
  - matching `allow` rules auto-answer native approvals through the existing runner command path;
  - matching `deny` rules auto-deny where the provider supports a denial response;
  - no match keeps the existing pending approval card behavior.
- Extract rule previews from Codex native payloads:
  - command prefix rules from `proposed_execpolicy_amendment` / safe prefix data;
  - file-change rules for workspace-root file write/edit grants;
  - network host/protocol rules from network permission context;
  - tool/provider rules from stable native method/tool identifiers.
- Add a browser rule-management surface: list active rules for the current workspace/provider and revoke/disable a rule.

## Interfaces

- New authenticated API:
  - `GET /api/approval-rules?workspace_id=...&provider_id=...`
  - `POST /api/approval-rules/{rule_id}/disable`
- Approval decision API keeps backward compatibility, but honors selected persistent option metadata when `option_id` identifies a rule-preview option.
- Universal approval events include rule-match/policy metadata so replay shows whether an approval was user-approved, policy-approved, policy-denied, or still pending.

## Test Plan

- DB tests for creating, matching, deduping, listing, and disabling policy rules.
- Policy tests for command prefix, workspace file-change, network host, and provider/tool matchers.
- Control-plane tests proving matched rules auto-answer approvals and unmatched requests still replay as pending.
- Runner approval tests confirming auto-resolved decisions still use the existing idempotent native delivery path.
- Web tests for rendering persistent approval options and revoking listed rules.
- Verification: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo check --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace`, then `web/` `npm run check`, `npm run lint`, `npm run test`, `npm run build`.

## Assumptions

- Persistent rules are scoped to user + workspace + provider by default.
- Agenter DB is the source of truth; no v1 mirroring into Codex native execpolicy files.
- Broad options are shown only from provider-supplied or conservatively derived rule previews.
- Revocation disables rules rather than deleting rows, preserving an audit trail.
- This needs a spec/ADR update because it changes approval persistence and security posture.

item.content: The docs and ADR are now added alongside the implementation. I’m starting verification from the format/check level, then focused tests, then the full frontend suite.

I’ll implement this end to end: docs/ADR first because this changes the approval security model, then the DB-backed policy surface, then control-plane auto-resolution and the web rule-management UI. I’ll keep the existing approval idempotency path intact so durable rules don’t create a second native-delivery mechanism.

item.content: I found the main insertion points: `publish_event` enriches and registers approval requests, `api/approvals.rs` owns user decisions, `policy.rs` is currently stateless, and `web/src/lib/chatEvents.ts` already maps canonical approval options. I’m going to add the rule model and repository tests first, then wire the runtime behavior against those tests.

Full workspace tests, frontend test/build, lint, fmt, clippy, and diff whitespace checks are green. I’m doing a final status review now so I can summarize exactly what changed and call out the existing dirty-worktree context accurately.

- Audit current approval and session seams for insertion points
- Add docs/ADR and DB model/repository tests first
- Implement durable approval rules and policy evaluation
- Wire approval decisions and auto-resolution through control plane
- Add browser rule listing/revocation UI and tests
- Run focused and broad verification
